title: Eating
blurb: Ordering, dietary restrictions, the check. The more polite phrases here, the better your trip will go.

phrases.use: The very first thing to say when seated.
Point at the menu or photo display. Works for anything visual.
Staff at small family restaurants love this question. Often leads to the off-menu specials.

phrases.romaji: eigo no menyuu wa arimasu ka?
phrases.en: Do you have an English menu?
phrases.use: Casual restaurants in tourist areas often do. The big chains always do.
phrases.note: AY-go no meh-NYU wa ah-ree-MAS ka.

phrases.use: Drinks almost always use a counter — hitotsu = one (thing).
Tap water is universally free and normal in Japan — 'mizu' is what you say.
Hand signal of crossing index fingers (X) is also widely understood.
Many smaller places won't split. Major chains often won't either. Bring cash as backup.
Fallback if they can't split. Useful at izakaya where one bill is the norm anyway.

phrases.en: Can I use a credit card?
phrases.use: Cash is still king in small Japan. Even when a place accepts cards, there may be a minimum (often ¥5,000).
Surprise saver — last-minute discovered no cards accepted is way less stressful if you ask first.
Useful but limited — Japanese dashi (stock) is often bonito-based. For serious allergies, see the next card.

phrases.en: I can't eat meat or fish.
phrases.use: More reliably understood than 'vegetarian'. Add 'tori mo' (chicken too) if you want to be extra clear.
Critical safety line. Say aloud and ideally write it on the allergy card from your phone. Cover allergens: peanuts, shellfish, soy, wheat, eggs, dairy, sesame.
phrases.note: Drop in the specific allergen: peanuts = 'piinattsu', shellfish = 'kai-rui', sesame = 'goma', soy = 'daizu', wheat = 'komugi', egg = 'tamago', milk = 'gyuunyuu'.

phrases.use: Many Japanese dishes use eggs as a hidden ingredient (mayo, glaze, batter). Ask before assuming.
Some regional ramen / curries are extremely spicy by default.
Hand-size portions are sometimes huge; 'sukuname' signals 'not as much'.
Most ramen / gyudon shops will upsize for free or for a small extra charge.
Most casual places are fine with it. Higher-end restaurants often aren't.

phrases.en: (said before eating) Thanks for the food
phrases.use: Even if you don't understand the rest, say this before eating. Locals love hearing it.
Said to the staff, or even to your empty plate. Tells the staff you appreciated it.
Staff genuinely light up hearing this. They may bring a free extra next time.
Refill / another round. The 'hitotsu' = one-of-thing counter.

phrases.romaji: kitsuen-seki desu ka, kin'en-seki desu ka?
phrases.use: Most restaurants still have a smoking section. Ask when seated.
phrases.note: KEE-tsoo-en sek-key / KEEN-en sek-key. The n is silent in 'kin'en'.
